content.subtitle: Relative Pronouns
items: who: to refer to people (and animals when we want to give them a personality)
which: to refer to things and concepts (and animals when we don't want to give them a personality)
whom: a formal word for who; as an object; must be used directly after a preposition
that: a more informal word for who, which, when, where, why; only used in defining relative clauses
when: to refer to time; = in/on/at which
where: to refer to place or situation; = in/at which
why: often after the word reason; = the reason for which; only used in defining relative clauses
whose: the possessive of who and which; can also come after a preposition
what: = the thing(s) which; only used in defining relative clauses

content.subtitle: Examples
items: There are a lot of people **who** hate having injections.
This is the prescription **which** the doctor gave me.
That's the consultant with **whom** I spoke.
I'll never forget the day **when** I broke my finger.
Harley Street, **where** she was born, is famous for its clinics.
And that's (the reason) **why** I wanted to become a vet.
There are several kids in my class **whose** parents are doctors.
**What** I don't understand is why she didn't take her pills.

content.subtitle: Prepositions in Relative Clauses
content.description: When we use a preposition with a relative pronoun, it is more formal to put the preposition before the pronoun.
examples: This is the medical encyclopedia **to which I referred.** (very formal)
This is the medical encyclopedia **which I referred to.** (less formal)

content.subtitle: Which Referring to a Whole Clause
content.description: Which can refer to the whole preceding clause, rather than just the preceding noun.
examples: She announced that she wanted to be a pathologist, **which really shocked us.** (= the announcement shocked us)

content.subtitle: Defining Relative Clauses
content.description: These tell us which one of a group of things/people we are talking about. The sentence doesn't usually make complete sense if we remove the relative clause.
items: We can use **that** instead of who/which/etc. This is more informal.
We don't use a comma or commas.
We can omit the relative pronoun if it is the object.
When, where and why can be omitted.
We cannot put a number or a determiner such as some, none, much and many before of which or of whom.
examples: That's the doctor **who did Karen's operation.**
That's the doctor **that did Karen's operation.**
I'll never forget **the day I broke my arm.** (when omitted)

content.subtitle: Non-Defining Relative Clauses
content.description: These simply give us more information about someone/something. The sentence makes complete sense if we remove the relative clause.
items: We cannot use **that** instead of who/which/etc.
We must use a comma or commas.
We cannot omit the relative pronoun.
We do not use why.
We cannot omit where and when.
We can put a number or a determiner such as some, none, much and many before of which or of whom.
examples: Dr Lake, **who has been working here for over ten years**, is a very experienced surgeon.
Harley Street, **where she was born**, is famous for its clinics.
I bought some drugs, **some of which** were expensive.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: To replace a relative clause
With prepositions and conjunctions
To explain the reason for something
To talk about actions happening at the same time
To talk about actions happening in sequence
As an alternative passive form
As an alternative conditional form

content.subtitle: Examples
items: She was the nurse **looking** after the patients at the time. (who was looking)
The boy **taken to hospital** was 13 years old. (who was taken)
After **giving blood**, I went home.
After **having given blood**, I went home.
**Being frightened of needles**, Tony was not looking forward to the injection.
**Having had several operations before**, Ali wasn't particularly nervous this time.
**Sitting** in the waiting room, I could hear the sound of the dentist drilling.
**Having found** an optician close to the office, I made an appointment for that evening.
**Given** an aspirin, I began to feel better. (when/because I was given an aspirin)
**Given** the chance, I'd definitely study pharmacology. (if I were given the chance)

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: When the participle clause doesn't have its own subject, the clause and the rest of the sentence must both refer to the same subject.
examples: ✓ **Standing** in the hot, crowded room, I began to feel dizzy. (= I was standing)
✗ **Standing** in the hot, crowded room, my head began to feel heavy. (= my head wasn't standing)

content.subtitle: Uses
items: To start a sentence
examples: **To be** a successful surgeon is the dream of many young children. (= It is the dream of many young children to be a successful surgeon.)
My job was **to give** the patients their lunch.

content.subtitle: Conjunctions and Phrases
items: although / though / even though
in spite of / despite (+ noun or -ing)
while / whereas
however
other phrases and structures: Try as he might, However hard..., Hard though/as..., Much as...

content.subtitle: Examples
items: Even though she'd put on sun cream, Tamsin got burnt.
Despite putting on sun cream, Tamsin got burnt.
While antibiotics are effective against bacteria, they do not work against viruses.
Penicillin is a powerful antibiotic. However, some people are allergic to it.
Try as he might, he couldn't put up with the pain.
However hard he tried, he couldn't put up with the pain.
Much as he tried, he couldn't put up with the pain.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: With in spite of / despite + -ing, both parts of the sentence must refer to the same subject.
items: Even if is used to emphasise that it doesn't matter if something happens or is true, another situation remains the same.
Even if suggests that something may or may not happen, whereas even though suggests that the action actually takes place.
examples: ✓ Even if they found a cure for cancer tomorrow, it would take several years before it was available.
✓ Even if she tried to give her an injection, she couldn't. (= She probably hasn't tried yet.)
✓ Even though she tried to give her an injection, she couldn't. (= She tried and was unsuccessful.)

sections.title: A: Relative Pronouns
sections.instructions: Write a relative pronoun in each gap. Do not use the word that.

items.sentence: Davina, ...... kids you met last weekend, works for Cooper's in the high street.
I'll never understand the reason ...... you decided to join the army.
The village ...... Alan grew up in is only a few miles from here.
Have you spoken to that guy ...... daughter might be able to help you with the contract?
Back in the 1970s, ...... I was a teenager, no one ...... I knew had a computer.
They've just announced that Beddington youth club, ...... we all used to hang out after school, is going to be turned into a casino.
Chicago's a city ...... I've always wanted to visit.
Do you remember the time ...... we got totally lost?
The cows ...... are in that field over there are Friesians.
Our cat Tibby, ...... loves being stroked all the time, is 16 years old today!
Bob wants to become a pop star, ...... seems like a totally unrealistic ambition to me.
The official to ...... you need to address your enquiry is temporarily unavailable.
...... spoils the film for me is the ending, ...... is just ridiculous.
Julian was the prefect ...... we all liked the best.

sections.title: B: Replacing with 'That'
sections.instructions: Tick the relative pronouns in exercise A which can be replaced by that.

items.sentence: Sentence 2: the reason ...... you decided
Sentence 3: The village ...... Alan grew up in
Sentence 5: no one ...... I knew
Sentence 7: a city ...... I've always wanted to visit
Sentence 8: the time ...... we got totally lost
Sentence 9: The cows ...... are in that field
Sentence 14: the prefect ...... we all liked
Sentence 1, 4, 6, 10, 11, 12, 13: cannot be replaced by that (non-defining clauses, after prepositions, or 'what')

sections.title: C: Omitted Relative Pronouns
sections.instructions: Circle the relative pronouns in exercise A which can be omitted.

items.sentence: Sentence 3: The village (which) Alan grew up in
Sentence 5: no one (whom) I knew
Sentence 7: a city (which) I've always wanted to visit
Sentence 8: the time (when) we got totally lost
Sentence 14: the prefect (whom) we all liked
Sentence 2: reason why - 'why' can be omitted
Sentence 1, 4, 6, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13: cannot be omitted

sections.title: D: Error Correction
sections.instructions: If a word or phrase in bold is correct, put a tick. If it is incorrect, rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: We took the dog **who we'd found** it abandoned in the street straight to the vet.
Would any boys **who's** surnames begin with M please raise your hands?
The day **which I** first met your mother was the best day of my life.
Alfredo thought for a second, and then began to write: 'To Whom It May Concern . .'.
The woman **who I spoke to** was extremely helpful.
The film **which I really want to see** it is the new one with Johnny Depp.
items.answer: which I really want to see

items.sentence: No, the hospital where **you were born there** was closed down quite a while ago.
Could you just give me one **reason why** you would say something like that?
The table **where we sat** at had a lovely view of the Acropolis.
That bothers me is why the police took so long to charge them.
The guy **whose car you just hit** it is a traffic warden!

sections.title: E: Commas in Relative Clauses
sections.instructions: Add commas to the sentences where necessary.

items.sentence: We were staying at the hotel that you recommended to us.
The film which was directed by Mel Gibson is actually in Hebrew.
items.answer: The film, which was directed by Mel Gibson, is actually in Hebrew.

items.sentence: The person who I look up to more than anyone else is my Uncle Geoffrey.
I bumped into Katherine the other day which was a strange coincidence.
items.answer: I bumped into Katherine the other day, which was a strange coincidence.

items.sentence: Is that the book which you were telling me about the other day?
The best man at my wedding who used to live in Germany has just moved to China.
items.answer: The best man at my wedding, who used to live in Germany, has just moved to China.

items.sentence: Have you got a suggestion which doesn't involve spending a lot of money?
Warwick University where I spent three wonderful years is one of the top ten universities in the country.
items.answer: Warwick University, where I spent three wonderful years, is one of the top ten universities in the country.

sections.title: F: Relative Clauses with Prepositions
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Do you really think the month ...... which you're born affects your character?
They declared war on Austria, the reason ...... which completely escapes me now.
There are five slices of pizza left, two ...... which we'd better save for Justin.
Is the day ...... which you arrive a Friday?
Josephine came to the rescue, of course, ...... which I'm eternally grateful.
There were 20 people at the meeting, some ...... whom I'd never met before.
The law needs to be strengthened, ...... which I mean the police need to be given new powers.
...... whom did you speak?
I looked at several possible presents, none ...... which were appropriate.
She studied in Berlin from 1916 to 1923, ...... which time many people still believed that women had no role in scientific research.
The region ...... which most of the wine is produced is to the south.
Jackson directed over 30 westerns, many ...... which are now available on DVD.

sections.title: G: Rewrite with Relative Clauses
sections.instructions: Rewrite as one sentence using a relative clause.

items.sentence: That tree is an oak. My grandfather planted it 60 years ago.
items.answer: That tree, which my grandfather planted 60 years ago, is an oak.

items.sentence: Tina and Charlie are having a party this Saturday. They're identical twins.
items.answer: Tina and Charlie, who are identical twins, are having a party this Saturday.

items.sentence: On the Friday, we spent most of the day sitting in the port. On the Friday, the ferry was delayed.
items.answer: On the Friday, when the ferry was delayed, we spent most of the day sitting in the port.

items.sentence: Barbara used to work in the Personnel Department. You met her at Libby's last Thursday.
items.answer: Barbara, whom you met at Libby's last Thursday, used to work in the Personnel Department.

items.sentence: Let's go to da Vinci's. They do a great pepper steak there.
items.answer: Let's go to da Vinci's, where they do a great pepper steak.

items.sentence: This book was once owned by Sir Francis Drake. It was given to me by my great aunt.
items.answer: This book, which was given to me by my great aunt, was once owned by Sir Francis Drake.

items.sentence: Why did Danny decide to enter the marathon? Danny's totally unfit.
items.answer: Why did Danny, who is totally unfit, decide to enter the marathon?

items.sentence: Guy is now engaged to my sister. His sister is married to my brother.
items.answer: Guy, whose sister is married to my brother, is now engaged to my sister.

sections.title: H: Participle Clauses
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: After **eating / having eaten** three hamburgers, it's no wonder you feel sick!
**Having been told / Telling** exactly what to do, I was pretty confident I wouldn't embarrass myself.
**Given / Giving** the opportunity, would you like to spend a year abroad?
Philip was the only person **keeping / who was keeping** the committee on track.
**Having seen / Seen** an advert in the paper, we decided to write off for more information.
The zoo-keeper **injured / having injured** in the incident is now in a stable condition.
**Not being / Not to be** a US citizen, Eleni knew it would be difficult to get a visa.
After **made / having made** a statement, I was allowed to leave.
**Shown / Having been shown** how to use the software, Adrian quickly started designing his logo.
**Listening / Having listened** from the top of the stairs, Jill and I could hear raised voices.
**To get / Getting** onto the property ladder is the aim of most young couples.
I was told that I was **to make / making** sure that the press would be there.

sections.title: I: Participle Clause Forms
sections.instructions: Write the verb in brackets in the correct form. Write only one or two words in each gap.

items.sentence: I'd love to get some work experience in an office, ...... (give) the chance.
After ...... (get) so bad a school report, you can't blame your parents for being angry.
The three men ...... (arrest) have not been named.
We were only given a room with a balcony after ...... (complain) to the manager.
...... (see) Arctic Monkeys play live before, Josh was desperate to get tickets to their next gig.
...... (swim) along the seabed, the divers were shocked to find a sunken car.
...... (apply) for the job, I forgot all about it until the letter came.
Is Bentley's the only department store ...... (start) its sale on Boxing Day?
...... (want) to create trouble, Dad didn't raise any objections.
It's Sophie's role ...... (wait) at the entrance on Tuesday evening to welcome the guests.
...... (shoot) in the arm, the man ran bleeding into the undergrowth.
...... (make) mistakes is only human!
1 ...... most scientists dismiss tales of out-of-body experiences on the operating table as dreams and delusions, there are some cases which seem to defy rational explanation.
2 There were serious complications, ...... , and her heart stopped beating.
3 Try ...... they might, the surgeon and anaesthetist were unable to resuscitate her.
4 ...... having been clinically dead, Margaret suffered no brain damage and no adverse side effects.
5 The story doesn't stop there, ...... .
6 ...... her never having been conscious in that room at any time, she described the room and the resuscitation attempt in great detail.
7 She said that ...... three of the walls were bright white, one was dark grey.
8 ...... the anaesthetist didn't – and couldn't – believe her, she checked.
9 ...... as they tried, no one at the hospital could explain how Margaret could have known about the book.
10 Even ...... one does not believe in a 'soul' or in life after death, it is difficult to explain what happened in that operating theatre when Margaret Frobisher's heart stopped beating.

sections.title: K: Sentence Transformation
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence: Despite having looked for it everywhere, Donald never found his contact lens. **he**
items.answer: Even **though he had looked** for it everywhere, Donald never found his contact lens.

items.sentence: The stadium won't be ready for the Olympics whether they start building tomorrow or not. **even**
items.answer: The stadium won't be ready for the Olympics **even if they start** building tomorrow.

items.sentence: The electricians are on strike but the BBC is still broadcasting. **spite**
items.answer: The BBC is still broadcasting **in spite of the fact that** the electricians are on strike.

items.sentence: Although no one gave us an invitation, we were still able to get into the club. **given**
items.answer: We were still able to get into the club in **spite of not being given** an invitation.

items.sentence: Dom couldn't finish the crossword despite really trying hard. **might**
items.answer: Try **as he might**, Dom couldn't finish the crossword.

items.sentence: Although Sarah shouted loudly, she wasn't able to get the audience to pay attention. **might**
items.answer: However **loudly she might shout**, Sarah wasn't able to get the audience to pay attention.

items.sentence: I couldn't persuade Tim in spite of my begging him to come with us. **as**
items.answer: Much **as I begged him**, I couldn't persuade Tim to come with us.

items.sentence: Despite our being late, we didn't think for a second we wouldn't make it on time. **though**
items.answer: Late **though we were**, we didn't think for a second we wouldn't make it on time.

sections.title: M: Cloze Text
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Most of us will suffer from a 'fracture', (1) ...... is the medical term for a broken bone, at some point in our lives.
A patient (2) ...... arm, let's say, is suspected of being broken will first be given an X-ray.
(3) ...... determined the extent of the fracture, the orthopaedic surgeon will decide on the most effective treatment.
For a fracture (4) ...... manipulation is required, (5) ...... may involve the patient being given a general anaesthetic while the bone is essentially re-broken.
Patients (6) ...... have more serious fractures may require 'fracture fixation', (7) ...... which pins and rods are connected to the bone to strengthen it.
(8) ...... , for simple fractures, a cast – often (9) ...... out of plaster of Paris or fibreglass – is all that is required.
(10) ...... a broken upper leg can take anything up to twelve weeks to heal, a broken toe can heal in just three weeks.
Their plaster cast (11) ...... off, the patient will almost certainly require physiotherapy to exercise a limb (12) ...... has been inactive for several weeks.
(13) ...... advances in medical technology, (14) ...... get a frail elderly person's fracture to heal is still remarkably difficult.
Even (15) ...... the fracture has been treated in the right way, it is quite possible that the bone will never fully heal.
